text: They receive rewards several times from specific co-players, but they never reciprocate with rewards of their own and they do not alter their contribution behavior, which is already at the maximum. There is no observable attempt to form a mutual-reward alliance or to differentially favor those who reward them versus those who do not. Their gratitude, if present, is not expressed through the available mechanisms. This suggests a style where they maintain their own cooperative standard independently of social approval or side payments, and do not translate being rewarded into explicit reciprocity via the reward tool. Whether they feel gratitude internally is unknown; behaviorally, they remain unchanged and non-reciprocal with respect to rewards.

text: They are rewarded a few times by other players despite not using the reward mechanism themselves. After receiving rewards, they do not change their pattern in any clearly affiliative or reciprocal way: they do not begin rewarding, they do not unambiguously increase contributions toward the apparent rewarders beyond what they were already doing, and they do not adopt the rewarders’ sanctioning style. Their contributions remain generally high with some variation, consistent with their prior trend, and there is no sign of a step-change in behavior following rewards. This suggests they experience rewards more as windfall income than as social signals that demand reciprocation or loyalty. They do not appear to update into a coalition mindset (e.g., “I should now support those who support me”). As a type, they accept positive treatment without reciprocating in kind, and they do not use reward receipts as cues for alliance-building or reputation management. Their gratitude level is behaviorally unknown; what can be inferred is that gratitude, if felt, does not translate into costly reciprocal action.

They continue to contribute fully and, in some cases, direct rewards toward players who themselves invest in others (such as frequent rewarders or vocal cooperators), which functions as indirect reciprocity. text: They receive repeated rewards across the game from several others. There is no evidence that they try to reciprocate directly to specific rewarders (for example, they do not clearly single them out with rewards in return). Nor do they explicitly thank rewarders in chat. Instead, they seem to respond by maintaining high cooperation and continuing to encourage the group as a whole. Their reciprocity is thus diffuse and group‑directed rather than dyadic: they “repay” being rewarded by persevering in prosocial actions and cooperative messaging, not by favor trading. text: They receive rewards a number of times, especially when they themselves continue not to contribute. They do not explicitly thank any specific rewarder by name, but they do respond in a way that broadly aligns with those players’ cooperative messaging: they echo calls for everyone to contribute, praise group improvements, and reward some of the same high-contributing players that others reward. This suggests indirect reciprocity rather than direct tit-for-tat: they do not seem to repay rewarders one-to-one, but they support the general pro-cooperation coalition through positive language and their own rewards. They never use being rewarded as a cue to start contributing themselves (except for a single late cooperative round, which is not clearly tied to receiving reward rather than to ongoing encouragement by a particular cooperator). They appear comfortable taking rewards while continuing to free-ride, showing low guilt or obligation to reciprocate via contribution. In terms of social stance, they treat being rewarded as reinforcement that they are part of the group, not as a binding commitment to change behavior.

This suggests they do not treat rewards as an obligation-creating gesture or as a cue to form a cooperative sub-coalition. They neither “pay back” nor “pay forward” rewards, and they also do not escalate their contributions in response to having been singled out positively. Their implicit stance toward rewarders is thus passive: they take the benefit but do not respond materially. Whether they privately feel gratitude is unknown; behaviorally, they remain non-reciprocal in the reward channel and only weakly conditional in contributions.

text: The player reliably notices and responds when others direct reward toward them. When they receive substantial reward, they subsequently maintain or increase their cooperative stance rather than exploiting the trust. They also show a tendency to direct rewards toward individuals who have previously supported them or behaved in ways beneficial to them, indicating some degree of positive reciprocity. However, their reciprocity is not automatic or symmetric; they do not simply “pay back” every unit of reward. Instead, they appear to integrate incoming rewards into a broader assessment of who is a valuable partner or cooperator. They are more likely to continue cooperating and occasionally reward those who treat them well, but they still weigh their own payoff and the broader group pattern. They do not retaliate against those who ignore or fail to reward them; absence of reward is not treated as an offense. This suggests a reciprocity norm that is appreciative rather than vengeful: they reward being rewarded, but they do not punish its absence. Their response pattern is consistent with alliance-building through mutual reinforcement rather than strict accounting.

text: This player is a pure reward free-rider: they accept rewards passively but never reciprocate. They receive rewards on multiple occasions from identifiable others, yet they never direct any rewards back to those benefactors, nor do they start rewarding anyone at all after being rewarded. There is no sign of gratitude signaling via the mechanism. They also do not adjust their contribution behavior in clear response to being rewarded. After receiving rewards, their contribution pattern remains largely unchanged in its free-riding orientation. This indicates that, for them, being rewarded does not create an obligation to “earn” that generosity via higher contributions or via reciprocal reward. Their reaction is best characterized as opportunistic acceptance rather than relational engagement. Thus, when rewarders are identifiable, this player does not use that information to form alliances or to signal reciprocity; they treat rewards as windfall income and maintain their original selfish strategy.

text: The player receives very little reward across the game and only modestly later on. They do not reliably repay those who reward them; there is no clear emergence of reciprocating pairs. Their own reward pattern does not pivot toward those who have rewarded them, nor do they maintain high rewards to such players once they reduce rewarding overall. This suggests low emphasis on direct reciprocity and alliance-building. Instead, their response to being rewarded is muted in behavior: their contribution path remains high whether or not they are personally recognized, and their decision to wind down rewarding appears more tied to the perceived ineffectiveness or cost of rewards in general, not to disappointment with specific rewarders. They seem to treat reward receipt as pleasant but non-central information, not as a binding social contract. Thus, any reciprocity is at most implicit: they continue to engage in cooperation with the group as a whole rather than forming clear bilateral alliances. There is no evidence of targeted payback rewards or punishments based specifically on who has favored them earlier. Their response to being rewarded seems to be general maintenance of cooperative behavior rather than personalized reciprocity.

text: When rewarded, this player does not reciprocate with direct rewards of their own. They do, however, often maintain or resume high contributions after receiving rewards, which may incidentally benefit the rewarders and the group at large. This suggests that while they do not practice explicit, targeted reciprocity via the reward tool, they may take reward as a weak signal that cooperating is worthwhile and adjust their contributions accordingly. They do not appear to interpret reward as an invitation to form a special bilateral alliance; there is no targeted payback. Instead, their reaction is generalized: they continue to participate actively in the public good. Their stance toward reward is thus appreciative but instrumental—they accept it and may let it reinforce cooperative play, yet they do not mirror it in kind. Overall, they are a “passive beneficiary” of reward rather than an active reciprocator: they do not build explicit reciprocal ties, but they remain engaged in group cooperation after being rewarded.

text: When they receive rewards from others, they do not visibly focus on direct, one-to-one repayment. Instead, their pattern is broad and inclusive: they continue to reward multiple others, including some who have not rewarded them. This suggests that they interpret being rewarded as confirmation of a cooperative norm rather than as a debt to a specific individual. They show no evidence of forming tight “alliances” based solely on bilateral reward exchange; their reward allocations do not collapse onto a small club. Rather, they maintain a group-centered style, spreading appreciation across multiple players, including fluctuating or improving contributors. They do not demonstrably differentiate in their contributions after being rewarded; they keep contributing very highly regardless. Thus, their response to being rewarded is best characterized as generalized reciprocity: rewards from anyone may reinforce their global commitment to being prosocial, encouraging them to keep sustaining cooperation and generosity toward the group at large, not only to the specific rewarders. Overall, this player types as a “reward-focused unconditional cooperator”: they strongly value group welfare, rely primarily on positive reinforcement over punishment, tolerate exploitation, and remain cooperative and generous even when others are inconsistent or when they themselves are punished. Their strategy is norm-driven and relationship-oriented, with high forgiveness and low concern for short-term individual payoff.

text: This player is repeatedly rewarded by several others but does not systematically reciprocate reward back to those specific individuals in any obvious pattern. Their reactions to being rewarded are indirect: they sometimes later reward players who are also generally cooperative or central to the cooperative cluster, but this does not unambiguously map onto “reward me → I reward you” reciprocity. There is no sign that they strongly adjust their contribution levels upward solely in response to being rewarded. Their contribution path remains volatile and more closely tied to conflict dynamics than to gratitude norms. Thus, they show weak direct reciprocity to positive actions; being rewarded does not reliably produce either increased cooperation or targeted return reward. However, they do appear to integrate positive experiences into a broader sense of who is “on their side”: some players they reward multiple times are also ones who do not heavily punish them. This may reflect a coarse form of alliance building, where they treat non‑aggressive or supportive peers more kindly, without strict one‑to‑one accounting. Their response to reward is therefore diffuse and symbolic rather than precise: they appreciate friendly behavior in aggregate but do not track and repay it in a finely tuned way. Overall, this player type is an emotionally sensitive, relationally focused participant: they can be cooperative, but their decisions are strongly influenced by perceived interpersonal alignment and conflict, leading to both generous rewards for perceived allies and intense, costly punishment for perceived adversaries.

text: This player is repeatedly rewarded by others, often substantially, yet their pattern of contributions and rewarding does not visibly change in a strategic, tit‑for‑tat manner. They neither single out particular rewarders for special reciprocal treatment nor withhold rewards from non‑rewarders; instead, they continue a broad, group‑level rewarding pattern. This suggests they are not primarily using rewards to build bilateral alliances or engage in direct reciprocity with identifiable partners. Nonetheless, they maintain and even escalate costly reward behavior over time, which is consistent with interpreting incoming rewards as confirmation that their cooperative/rewarding norm is shared or appreciated. Their reaction can be described as generalized reciprocity or group‑level gratitude: rather than targeting specific benefactors, they “pay it forward” to the group. Because we do not see them sharply increasing or decreasing rewards to any individual immediately after being rewarded by that individual, fine‑grained contingent reciprocity is unknown or low. Overall, they respond to being rewarded by staying committed to a high‑cooperation, high‑reward strategy, but not by forming narrow favoritism patterns toward particular rewarders.

They do not build observable “alliances” with rewarders via preferential treatment in punishment or reward, nor do they respond to receiving rewards by relaxing enforcement toward defectors. Their behavior suggests that they treat rewards as incidental “extra income” rather than as relational signals that demand reciprocity. Any internal sense of gratitude is unknown; behaviorally, they are non-reciprocal with respect to positive incentives while being responsive in the sense that they maintain high contributions that already match the cooperative expectations implied by reward. In summary, this player’s type is: norm-focused, high-contribution cooperator who uses targeted punishment to enforce cooperation, is non-retaliatory when punished, and largely ignores the relational dimension of rewards.

text: They do not visibly condition their contributions on how much reward they receive; full cooperation persists regardless of whether they are heavily rewarded, moderately rewarded, or not rewarded at all. There is some evidence that they adjust their own reward behavior when their incoming rewards drop sharply: they briefly stop rewarding altogether after receiving no reward despite full cooperation, then later resume high reward spending once reward flows to them pick up again. This pattern suggests that they notice and care about being appreciated, and that they may use reward both as positive reinforcement and as a reciprocal signal toward those who treat them generously. However, because individual-level reward matrices are not provided, the exact structure of any reciprocal alliances is unknown and inference about specific tit-for-tat relationships remains low confidence. Overall, they appear to value mutual rewarding and may be somewhat sensitive to feeling ignored, but they do not let this affect their baseline cooperative contributions.

text: The player receives modest but recurring rewards from others across the game. Their own reward behavior, however, does not track one‑for‑one with fluctuations in rewards received. There are intervals where they spend heavily on rewards while receiving relatively little, then later reduce or suspend their own rewarding even though they still receive something. This dissociation implies that they are not acting as a simple reciprocal partner who mirrors reward input, nor as a narrow alliance‑builder focused only on those who reward them. Instead, their response pattern fits a norm‑driven or group‑oriented type: they continue full cooperation regardless of how much they are personally rewarded, and they deploy reward according to a broader cooperative strategy rather than as payback to specific benefactors. There is no clear evidence of targeted retaliation (withholding reward from previous non‑rewarders) or of forming tight mutual‑reward cliques; if such structures exist, they are not inferable from the provided information. Overall, being rewarded does not appear necessary to sustain their prosocial behavior, and changes in how much they are rewarded do not visibly shift their contributions or their baseline willingness to use the reward tool.

text: The player regularly receives reward from others and continues to cooperate fully throughout, so at minimum they do not respond to being rewarded by reducing their own contributions or ceasing to reward. They also sometimes increase or sustain substantial reward spending during stretches where they themselves are being rewarded, which is consistent with reciprocal reinforcement, but exact dyadic reciprocity (rewarding specific prior rewarders) is unobservable from the transcript. Thus, whether they target their benefactors specifically is unknown. At the aggregate level, they appear comfortable participating in a mutual‑reward environment: they keep giving when they are also receiving and do not exploit incoming rewards as a reason to free‑ride. Overall they behave like a prosocial, positively reciprocal type, but the fine‑grained pattern of alliance building or partner selection among rewarders remains unknown.

text: The player receives substantial positive reinforcement from others throughout the game, but their response is restrained and not strongly contingent. Being rewarded does not lead to escalated or systematic reciprocal rewarding; nor does a lack of incoming reward cause them to reduce contributions or cease cooperation. Their core cooperative behavior appears stable regardless of how much they are personally rewarded. This suggests they interpret incoming rewards more as validation of their cooperative identity than as a signal that demands precise tit‑for‑tat responses. They do occasionally reward while being rewarded themselves, which could indicate some weak form of generalized reciprocity or gratitude, but the pattern is not tight enough to infer targeted alliance building with specific rewarders. When rewarders are identifiable, they do not clearly engage in “you reward me, I reward you” cycles; instead, they continue to behave as a broadly prosocial group member rather than a coalition‑focused strategist. In sum, they appreciate and benefit from being rewarded (as evidenced by continued high cooperation) but do not structurally alter their strategy to reciprocate or leverage those rewards in a strategic, alliance‑driven way.

text: The player is consistently rewarded by others, indicating that they are perceived as cooperative or prosocial by at least some peers. Their own continued cooperation and repeated use of rewards, even after receiving variable amounts of reward, suggests they respond to being rewarded by reinforcing their cooperative identity rather than by switching to pure self-interest. There is evidence of gratitude-like behavior rather than narrow self-maximization: they maintain or increase contributions in phases when they are rewarded, and they do not sharply cut back on rewards when their own incoming rewards dip. This implies that their response to identifiable rewarders is not purely contingent; they are not simply matching what they get. Instead, they act like someone who values being part of a mutually supportive cluster and is willing to keep investing even if reciprocity is imperfect. That said, the pattern does not clearly show one-to-one reciprocal “alliances” with specific individuals; the data does not reveal target identities, so whether they specifically favor prior rewarders is unknown. What is observable is a generalized reciprocal stance: they respond to the overall presence of rewarding and cooperation in the group by continuing to contribute and to reward, behaving as a stable, positively reciprocal type rather than a strict conditional trader of rewards.

text: They sometimes receive rewards from others despite contributing nothing, yet they never reciprocate toward those rewarders or adjust their own behavior in response. Identifiability of rewarders does not lead them to form alliances, reward back, or shift from free-riding to at least partial cooperation. Their response to being rewarded is purely passive: they accept the additional income but do not treat it as a signal to coordinate or to change strategy. This pattern suggests that they either interpret rewards as exploitable generosity or do not integrate them into their strategic reasoning. Any feelings of gratitude, if present, do not manifest behaviorally. Their type is thus non-reciprocal: they neither reward cooperative contributors nor those who personally benefit them, and they maintain a strictly unilateral, self-regarding strategy despite repeated positive treatment from others.

text: The player frequently receives reward from others and seems to interpret being rewarded as a confirmation of shared norms rather than as an invitation to immediate reciprocal gifting. They generally maintain their cooperative behavior regardless of whether they are heavily rewarded, lightly rewarded, or not rewarded in a given round, suggesting that their contributions are not contingent on short-run reciprocity. There are rounds where they receive reward but do not respond by spending on reward themselves, and rounds where they spend significantly on reward even when they receive little or none, indicating that strict tit-for-tat reciprocity is not their guiding rule. At the same time, their willingness to keep contributing and to invest in reward despite occasional low incoming reward suggests they feel aligned with other cooperators and are comfortable acting as a reliable ally in a loose coalition of high contributors. When they ramp up their own rewarding late in the game, this can be read as an attempt to acknowledge or support those who have consistently rewarded or cooperated with them, but the available data do not allow clear identification of specific dyadic reciprocation patterns; whether they target prior rewarders in particular is unknown. Overall, they respond to being rewarded primarily by maintaining high cooperation and by demonstrating, over time, that they are a stable, norm-driven cooperator rather than an opportunistic reciprocator.

text: The player frequently receives rewards from others, including in both cooperative and free-riding rounds. They continue to use rewards themselves even after being rewarded multiple times, indicating that they interpret incoming rewards as compatible with their existing strategy rather than as a reason to stop spending on others. In that sense, they are not discouraged from rewarding just because they already benefit from others’ generosity. Their contribution behavior does not lock into a purely reciprocal cycle where being rewarded always triggers cooperation; they sometimes free-ride despite having been rewarded recently, and they sometimes cooperate without obvious immediate reward-based incentive. This implies that they are not a simple “direct reciprocity” type. Instead, they treat rewards as part of a broader environment: they remain generally willing to reward others but do not rigidly match their own contribution level or reward spending to the exact amount of reward they receive. There is no clear evidence of targeted alliance-building with specific rewarders versus non-rewarders, since the transcript does not show to whom they direct rewards. Whether they preferentially reward those who have rewarded them is unknown. However, their continued readiness to both give and receive rewards suggests that they are comfortable in a mutually reinforcing reward culture and see themselves as an active participant in it rather than a pure free-rider on others’ reward efforts.

text: The player is repeatedly rewarded by others yet does not change their behavior in response. Their contribution level remains maximally cooperative both before and after receiving rewards, and they do not begin to reciprocate with reward even as they become a clear beneficiary of others’ generosity. This suggests limited use of reward as a signal for alliance building or targeted reciprocity. They do not appear to adopt a “partners” strategy where they mutually reinforce a core of cooperators through reciprocal rewards. Instead, they act as if rewards are incidental bonuses: appreciated or at least accepted (inferred from continuing normal play), but not behaviorally consequential. There is no evidence of gratitude signaling through “rewarding back,” and no shift in strategy that would indicate they interpret reward as a cue to coordinate or differentiate between group members. Their stance toward identifiable rewarders is behaviorally neutral: they continue with their own unconditional cooperation policy and ignore the opportunity to create or reinforce coalitions via the reward channel.

text: This player is consistently rewarded by others, often to a relatively high degree, which fits their pattern of unwavering full contribution. In response, they keep contributing fully throughout, showing that being rewarded reinforces rather than undermines their cooperative stance. Their reward behavior suggests at least some reciprocity: they continue to spend on rewards after receiving them, and there are phases where they reward more heavily following periods of substantial incoming rewards. It is likely that they direct some of their rewards toward those who have previously rewarded them and who continue to cooperate, implying a tendency toward alliance building among high contributors, although the exact mapping of targets is unknown. When incoming rewards temporarily fall or become modest, they do not retaliate or reduce their own cooperation, but they sometimes scale back their reward spending, which could reflect a mild conditional component to their gratitude signaling: they prefer to reward those who signal support to them, while still upholding their own cooperative standard regardless. They do not appear spiteful or exclusionary; rather, they act as a loyal, high‑effort partner toward those they view as fellow cooperators and supportive peers, using rewards as an expression of appreciation and as a means to sustain mutually reinforcing relationships.

text: The player reliably receives rewards across many rounds and appears responsive to this social feedback. Once others start rewarding them, they converge quickly to consistent high contributions and maintain them. This is consistent with a sensitivity to approval and a desire to live up to the cooperative expectations reflected in incoming rewards. Although individual reward links cannot be fully reconstructed, the player’s own reward spending pattern suggests a tendency toward reciprocation and alliance reinforcement: when they are part of a strongly cooperative and rewarding subgroup, they increase their own rewarding; when incoming rewards decline or the cooperative climate weakens, their spending on rewards sometimes falls, then rises again when cooperative signals strengthen. This points to a norm of “support those who support cooperation,” rather than indiscriminate generosity. They treat being rewarded as confirmation that their cooperative strategy is appropriate and worth sustaining, not as a reason to cash in on goodwill by defecting. There is no evidence of retaliatory behavior toward anyone who fails to reward them; instead, they focus on positively reinforcing cooperators. Overall, they behave as a reciprocity-oriented, alliance-building cooperator: responsive to recognition, inclined to reward those who uphold group cooperation, and willing to maintain cooperative and rewarding behavior as long as they perceive a cooperative core in the group.

They do not reliably respond to incoming rewards by sending rewards back, even when the mechanics allow them to identify and selectively reward contributors. Their contribution level remains high regardless of whether they are being rewarded more or less, suggesting that they do not condition their cooperation on receiving positive reinforcement. The late, large reward outlay does not align in a simple one-to-one way with previously received rewards and is not followed by a new pattern of reciprocity. Thus there is no strong evidence of targeted alliance building or tit-for-tat gratitude toward specific rewarders. Their response to being rewarded is mainly behavioral stability rather than strategic adjustment: they keep cooperating at roughly the same high level and mostly ignore the reward channel. In sum, when rewarders are identifiable, this player does not systematically reciprocate, does not appear to form explicit reward-based alliances, and treats received rewards more as incidental benefits than as obligations or signals to which they must respond.

text: They repeatedly receive rewards from others while maintaining full contributions both before and after those rewards. However, they do not reciprocate with rewards, even toward those likely responsible for rewarding them, and they do not shift their punishment pattern based on who rewarded them. There is no observable attempt to build reciprocal reward “alliances” or to favor particular partners. The most consistent reading is that they interpret rewards as confirmation of a shared norm rather than as part of a reciprocity contract. Their continued cooperation in the presence of rewards is not diagnostic, since they cooperate anyway; the key signal is their lack of reward-giving in return. They show gratitude, if at all, only implicitly through sustained cooperation, not via direct costly reciprocity. In short, they are stable cooperators who respond to being rewarded by continuing to behave cooperatively, but they do not engage in strategic or reciprocal rewarding of specific individuals and do not appear to treat rewards as obligations to repay.

text: They receive rewards from others multiple times but never reciprocate with reward to anyone. This makes them a non-reciprocator in the explicit reward channel, even though they are a consistent cooperator in the contribution stage. Their contribution behavior does not visibly change after being rewarded; they are already at maximum cooperation and remain there. Thus, being rewarded neither escalates nor diminishes their cooperation. They also do not respond by selectively rewarding those who rewarded them or who cooperated, so they do not use rewards to acknowledge benefactors, to form coalitions, or to “return the favor” through the same medium. Their response to reward is therefore: maintain existing cooperative behavior, absorb the benefit, and remain neutral and inactive in the reward system itself. Overall, this player’s type is: a principled, rigid full cooperator who ignores both punishment and reward mechanisms, maintains cooperation regardless of others’ actions or the game’s horizon, and does not engage in explicit reciprocity through institutionalized sanctions or bonuses. Their incentives appear aligned with internal norms of “always cooperate” rather than with payoff-driven adjustment or strategic use of social tools.

text: They receive small rewards at least twice. When rewarded, they respond with brief, friendly acknowledgement toward the rewarding cooperator, thanking them. They do not reciprocate via monetary rewards, but they continue to cooperate and stay aligned with the cooperative coalition. This indicates low monetary reciprocity but normal social reciprocity: they use words of appreciation rather than coins. They do not form explicit alliances with rewarders beyond general in-group camaraderie, and their punishment choices remain focused on norm violators rather than on favoring rewarders. --- Type synopsis: “Stubborn Cooperative Enforcer with Spiteful Memory” This player is a consistent, unconditional full cooperator who strongly values group efficiency and adherence to a simple fairness rule: everyone should always contribute everything. They combine that with a willingness to pay for punishment of those who violate norms or disrupt the process. Their strategy is not payoff-maximizing in a narrow sense; they willingly sacrifice some earnings to punish past misbehavior, even after the offender returns to cooperation. They are socially engaged, outspoken, and easily drawn into group emotions. They align with the dominant cooperative majority, amplify group pressure on defectors, and use direct, sometimes harsh language to shame and target perceived offenders. They rely exclusively on punishment, not reward, for norm enforcement; and although they appreciate being rewarded, they do not reciprocate monetarily. They hold a long memory and a retributive streak: once someone is labeled as a liar, troll, or sneaky cooperator, they continue to punish that player over multiple later rounds. However, they never respond to misbehavior by withdrawing their own cooperation; instead, they maintain full contributions and handle norm violations via punitive action and social criticism.

text: They are explicitly grateful and reinforcing toward those who reward them; when they receive rewards, they immediately thank the giver by name in chat, sometimes more than once. This suggests a strong tendency toward social reciprocity at the communication level, even though they do not necessarily reciprocate with counter‑rewards (they never directly reward the player who consistently rewards them). Instead, they respond with praise, public appreciation, and affiliative comments (“you’re awesome!”). This pattern points to an alliance‑building style that relies on words and ongoing cooperation rather than matching material transfers. They also generalize goodwill toward frequent rewarders, interacting with them warmly and supporting their cooperative norms. Whether they would materially reciprocate in a different environment with better reward efficiency is unknown; in this setting, their reciprocity remains primarily verbal and relational rather than financial.

text: They receive rewards multiple times and respond by continuing to contribute fully and to participate positively in group chat. They do not explicitly thank specific rewarders, but they acknowledge and reciprocate the cooperative norm at the group level (e.g., endorsing that they are a team, maintaining full contributions, supporting other cooperators in chat). There is no evidence that receiving rewards changes their punishing behavior: they continue to punish defectors aggressively and do not spare previously punished targets because of receiving any reward themselves. They also do not attempt to form explicit bilateral alliances with their rewarders through targeted payback or protection. Overall, they treat rewards as a pleasant but non-strategic signal of group cooperation; they remain committed to their own norm-enforcement and high-contribution strategy regardless of who rewards them.

text: When other players reward or support them, they respond with banter and mild appreciation rather than explicit gratitude or reciprocal monetary behavior. They acknowledge social gestures (like people calling them “wolf daddy,” welcoming them when they contribute, or apologizing for over-punishing), and their behavior softens in those periods: they increase contributions and reduce provocative talk about sabotaging the group. However, this reciprocity is informal and emotional rather than instrumental. They do not respond to being rewarded with counter-rewards or targeted cooperation; instead, they shift their overall stance from antagonistic to more cooperative and playful. Their alliances are social (shared jokes, nicknames, back-and-forth teasing) rather than enforced through transfers. In short, they are sensitive to social affirmation but do not operationalize that sensitivity via the reward tool; their reciprocity is behavioral (more willing to contribute) and conversational, not monetary.

Their main reaction pattern is indirect: they maintain or even intensify cooperation and occasionally send rewards to a consistently cooperative player, but this looks more like reinforcing a generally good group member than direct tit-for-tat reciprocity. Their cooperation is already maximal, so there is little room for upward behavioral adjustment. Thus, while they accept rewards passively, they do not use them to form alliances or to communicate gratitude; their reciprocity is behavioral only in the sense that they keep contributing fully to the group as a whole, not specifically to those who reward them.

text: They appear to track who is willing to spend on rewards and tend to position themselves to benefit from those players rather than forming a strict reciprocal alliance. When they are rewarded, they do not clearly and consistently “return the favor” in a targeted way; any reciprocity is weak or inconsistent. Instead, they seem to adopt an opportunistic stance toward rewarders: if someone is demonstrably generous with rewards, they continue to behave in ways that keep themselves an attractive target (often contributing enough to look cooperative, sometimes spending small amounts on others), but they do not reliably funnel their own reward budget back to that same individual. Their behavior suggests that they treat being rewarded as confirmation that others are willing to transfer surplus, and they then continue to play in ways that maintain or increase that surplus flow to themselves rather than investing in symmetrical reciprocity. Whether they hold explicit beliefs about “alliances” is unknown, but their pattern fits a payoff-maximizing, weak-reciprocity type more than a strong, loyalty-driven reciprocator.

text: The player does not exhibit clear, consistent direct reciprocity toward specific rewarders. When they are personally rewarded, they do not reliably respond by increasing their contribution, nor by redirecting rewards back to the same individual in a stable, alliance-like pattern. Any occasional alignment between being rewarded and later rewarding that same person is intermittent and not strong enough to infer a robust tit-for-tat dynamic. Instead, their responses to being rewarded look weakly coupled to the identity of the rewarder. They do not use reward to lock in bilateral alliances or to “thank” particular partners. Their main adjustment channels (both contributing and rewarding) appear more tied to the general contribution environment than to one-on-one relationships. Psychologically, this points to a more “market-like” than relational orientation: they react to the overall pattern of cooperation rather than cultivating personal reciprocity bonds. Whether they consciously ignore personal favors or simply prioritize group-level signals over individual ones is unknown.

There are no explicit “thank you” messages or alliance-building statements in response to being rewarded. Their chat contributions after being rewarded remain general and humorous rather than relational or strategic. They also do not advertise that they reward others; when they spend heavily on rewards, they do not announce this or leverage it as a social signal. This suggests they are relatively private or modest about their reward behavior and not trying to form explicit coalitions based on reward exchange. When other players discuss rewards more broadly (e.g., complaining about not being rewarded or talking about whom they are rewarding), this player does not engage much in that meta-conversation. They neither claim entitlement to rewards nor criticize the distribution. Compared to many PGG players, they appear low in explicit reciprocity signaling: they probably do reciprocate at an aggregate level (cooperating and sometimes rewarding), but not in a trackable dyadic way. Overall, their response to reward is muted and impersonal: they accept it as part of the cooperative environment without treating it as the basis for personal alliances, targeted reciprocity, or public gratitude.

text: They are receptive and appreciative when rewarded. When another player thanks them for rewards they have given, they respond warmly and acknowledge the gratitude, reinforcing a friendly reciprocal relationship. When they themselves feel under-rewarded, they mention it openly but in a light, non-accusatory way, which prompts others to correct that by rewarding them. This indicates they attend to reward flows and expect some degree of reciprocity or at least recognition, but they do not escalate or threaten defection when under-rewarded. Their pattern suggests “soft reciprocity”: they appreciate and remember rewarders, treat rewards as signals of alliance and shared norm adherence, and are willing to keep cooperating regardless, while using chat to gently signal when reciprocity seems uneven. They are comfortable in a role where they both give and receive rewards, and interpret the mechanism as a way to mark and maintain cooperative relationships rather than a strict accounting system.

Their few verbal responses related to rewards are general acknowledgments of niceness or appreciation of the cooperative environment, rather than explicit reciprocal pledges or alliance-building. They also do not publicly link their own decisions to reward receipt (they never say they will reward someone because that person rewarded them). Nonetheless, their later generous use of the reward mechanism is consistent with a diffuse form of reciprocity: they reward the group of cooperators and join the emergent “mutual appreciation” culture, rather than creating narrow bilateral alliances. When others mention giving rewards “to the non greedy” or talk about reward distribution, they respond positively but without trying to steer the targeting. Overall, their response to being rewarded is to remain fully cooperative, to keep participating socially, and eventually to start rewarding more themselves, but in an inclusive, non-calculating way rather than through explicit tit-for-tat or coalition-building.

Their acknowledgments are diffuse rather than targeted: they thank “bird” or engage in friendly banter after being rewarded, but they do not explicitly pledge or implement reciprocal reward behavior toward specific individuals. They also do not appear to form explicit “alliances” with particular reward-givers; their social appreciation is broad and group-focused. Their main way of “responding” is to maintain a positive group atmosphere and to keep playing along with the cooperative norm. This suggests they interpret rewards more as social nice-to-haves or compliments than as binding obligations or as signals that should change their own side-payment strategy. In summary, as a type, this player is a mostly stable cooperator who values group harmony, enjoys light social interaction, and is willing to sustain high contributions with minimal strategic policing. They free-ride on the reward mechanism while maintaining a prosocial image through talk, occasional humor about greed and bullying, and continued alignment with the cooperative majority.
section_text: When they receive rewards, they respond with general gratitude and friendliness in chat but do not translate that into costly reciprocity. Their acknowledgments are diffuse rather than targeted: they thank “bird” or engage in friendly banter after being rewarded, but they do not explicitly pledge or implement reciprocal reward behavior toward specific individuals. They continue to contribute cooperatively, which indirectly benefits everyone, including rewarders, but they do not alter their reward-spending pattern in response to being personally rewarded. They also do not appear to form explicit “alliances” with particular reward-givers; their social appreciation is broad and group-focused. Their main way of “responding” is to maintain a positive group atmosphere and to keep playing along with the cooperative norm. This suggests they interpret rewards more as social nice-to-haves or compliments than as binding obligations or as signals that should change their own side-payment strategy. In summary, as a type, this player is a mostly stable cooperator who values group harmony, enjoys light social interaction, and is willing to sustain high contributions with minimal strategic policing. They free-ride on the reward mechanism while maintaining a prosocial image through talk, occasional humor about greed and bullying, and continued alignment with the cooperative majority.

text: They show gratitude and social acknowledgment when reward issues are raised in chat. When another player complains about not being rewarded, they explicitly confirm that the player has now been rewarded, using the channel to signal that they have just directed rewards toward them. They do not overtly lobby for personal rewards, nor do they attempt to form explicit reward alliances; instead, their reaction is to reward more broadly rather than to demand reciprocity. When they themselves receive rewards, they do not single out specific rewarders for thanks, but they continue to behave cooperatively and often remain a net giver of rewards afterward, which is consistent with a diffuse reciprocity orientation: they “pay it forward” to the group rather than directly paying back particular individuals. There is no evidence of them punishing non-rewarders or shifting contributions downward as retaliation for being overlooked. Overall, they respond to being rewarded with continued cooperation and sustained generosity, prioritizing group-level goodwill over bilateral accounting.

text: They do not explicitly thank or acknowledge specific rewarders, even when they receive substantial rewards. There are no direct “thanks” messages or alliance-building statements aimed at identifiable benefactors. Their one playful comment about the free-riding ape is not directed at any reward relationship. This lack of verbal reciprocity does not mean they ignore rewards behaviorally: given their steady willingness to spend on rewards, it is plausible (though unconfirmed) that they partly reciprocate to those who have rewarded them, but this cannot be established from chat alone. Socially, they respond to being rewarded with continued cooperation and continued use of the reward system, not with explicit gratitude, coalition talk, or attempts to coordinate mutual rewarding. Overall, their response pattern looks like quiet, action-based prosociality rather than overt relational management; they appreciate the cooperative environment enough to keep contributing and rewarding but do not outwardly curate reputational ties.

text: They receive rewards in many rounds but never explicitly acknowledge or thank specific rewarders in chat. They also do not shift their behavior in response to being rewarded—there is no increase in communication, no movement toward using the reward mechanism themselves, and no visible attempt to create reciprocal reward loops. Their main “reciprocal” act is to continue fully contributing and advocating for all‑in cooperation, which benefits all cooperators, rewarders included, but this is not targeted at rewarders per se. They do occasionally respond positively to group developments that are linked to others’ pro‑social actions (for example, when a previous low contributor finally cooperates, they respond approvingly), but this is indirect and not framed as payback to rewarders. Overall, their response to being rewarded is behaviorally inert: they accept rewards passively and stay on their pre‑existing cooperative path without engaging in explicit gratitude or strategic reciprocity.

text: Their response to being rewarded is understated and pragmatic. They occasionally note in chat that they haven’t been rewarded much and then continue to reward others anyway, which indicates low entitlement and a weak demand for strict reciprocity. There is no clear evidence they systematically direct more rewards back to those who reward them; because identities are visible, they could do so, but their high, broad spending pattern suggests they are not narrowly favoring particular partners. Their behavior implies that rewards function more as a confirmation that the group is cooperative rather than as a personal debt marker. They do not publicly thank specific rewarders or attempt to form explicit bilateral alliances based on reward exchange. When they do receive rewards, they accept them silently and maintain their high contribution level, suggesting they interpret rewards as encouragement to stay on the cooperative track rather than as a reason to change strategy. Overall, their reciprocity is generalized and group-focused rather than dyadic: they respond to a cooperative, rewarding environment by staying generous to the group, not by tracking and repaying individuals one-to-one.

text: They receive occasional rewards but exhibit no visible behavioral change in response. They neither increase contributions (which are already at the maximum almost all the time) nor start using rewards themselves. They also do not explicitly thank or align with specific rewarders in chat; any gratitude is either absent or too subtle to detect. Their cooperation level is already saturated, so rewards cannot move them upward; they instead appear to treat rewards as incidental windfalls rather than as cues for alliance-building or reciprocal favor-exchange. They do not reciprocate rewards through direct rewarded-return to the same individuals, nor do they adopt a broader “reward cooperators” norm in later decisions. This indicates a type that is stable in their cooperative strategy regardless of receiving positive incentives: they are intrinsically or normatively committed to full contribution and do not condition that on being rewarded.

text: They are sometimes rewarded by others but do not show a clear, traceable pattern of direct reciprocal rewarding toward specific individuals who reward them. There is at least one instance of receiving reward while not giving any in return that round, and there is no consistent evidence of immediate payback to identifiable rewarders. This suggests that while they appreciate being rewarded enough to keep contributing and rewarding in general, their reward decisions are not obviously driven by narrow reciprocity to specific partners. Instead, they seem to base their reward choices more on others’ contribution behavior or general role in the group’s cooperation level than on whether those others have personally transferred reward to them. text: The player appears sensitive to being rewarded but does not alter their contribution pattern in response; they remain fully cooperative whether they are rewarded or ignored. Any effect of being rewarded manifests, if at all, in their use of the reward mechanism, not in their own contribution level. When they receive rewards from others, they do not clearly shift into a stable mutual-reward alliance with those specific individuals. After being rewarded, they sometimes increase or sustain substantial reward outflows in general, but they also at other times continue rewarding even when they are not rewarded back, and in some phases they reward heavily without receiving any reward. This makes direct reciprocity toward specific rewarders uncertain or at best weak: there is no clear one-to-one matching of “you rewarded me, so I now reward you.” Instead, their behavior fits an impersonal, norm-focused pattern: they appear to reward people based on current cooperative behavior rather than on whether those players previously rewarded them. They do not seem to retaliate against non-rewarders, nor to withdraw cooperation when rewards to them stop. Gratitude, if present, is not expressed as selective, partner-specific reciprocity but rather as maintaining or amplifying a general culture of rewarding cooperators. In terms of social style, they look like a principled cooperator who values stable, high-contribution norms over bilateral exchange relationships. They are willing to keep cooperating and often to keep rewarding even when that stance is not materially reciprocated, suggesting a personality that is persistent, norm-driven, and relatively insensitive to personal payoff inequalities, while still conditionally allocating rewards to reinforce cooperative behavior and withhold them from defectors.

text: They are repeatedly rewarded by others yet never reciprocate via the reward mechanism. This asymmetry indicates that, while they accept and presumably value the extra payoff, they do not adopt a tit-for-tat or alliance-building strategy in the reward stage. Their continued high cooperation despite receiving rewards suggests that their contribution rule is not driven by these transfers; they cooperate even in situations where no rewards arrive and do not increase or decrease cooperation systematically in response to being rewarded. Thus, they do not use reward inflows as a salient signal to adjust strategy. Their attitude toward rewarders appears instrumentally passive: they benefit from others’ generosity without mirroring it, but also without reducing cooperation when rewards are absent. This points to low reciprocity specifically in the reward domain, combined with a stable cooperative disposition in the contribution domain. Whether they interpret rewards as personal approval, as mere bonus payoff, or as noise is unknown; behaviorally, they do not act as if they are in an identifiable partnership with particular rewarders, and they do not attempt to cultivate such partnerships through targeted responses.

text: This player is responsive but not mechanically reciprocal toward identifiable rewarders. They clearly notice reward directed at them: when they receive reward while also observing cooperative partners, they continue contributing and are again willing to spend on rewards themselves. This is consistent with a desire to sustain cooperative relationships and to signal appreciation to those who treat them well. However, their reciprocity is imperfect and context-dependent. They do not adopt a strict tit-for-tat rule in either contributions or rewards: they at times defect (contribute nothing) despite the continued presence of cooperative partners who have previously rewarded them, and they sometimes continue rewarding even when the balance of rewards they receive is not particularly favorable. This indicates that while they care about being rewarded and are influenced by it, they do not tie their behavior one-to-one to any single partner. They seem to treat reward as a group-level signal rather than a purely dyadic contract. Receiving reward appears to reassure them that “someone” values cooperative behavior, which supports their own willingness to contribute and to reward others in the network. When they experience phases of no or low reward, they become more likely to experiment with free-riding, but they do not permanently abandon cooperation. In social terms, they present as a moderately prosocial, status- and recognition-sensitive player: they like to be acknowledged, they respond positively and in kind at the aggregate level, but they also maintain autonomy and are willing to deviate from pure reciprocity when they feel that norms or rewards are insufficient or unstable. Their behavior suggests a personality that is cooperative but not self-sacrificial, attuned to social feedback, and willing to strategically adjust both contribution and reward as a function of how appreciated and supported they feel.

text: The player repeatedly accepts rewards without changing their behavior in a pro-social direction, indicating very weak reciprocity. Even when they are rewarded while actively free-riding on others’ cooperation, they do not increase their own contributions, nor do they begin to reward those who rewarded them. When they themselves briefly cooperate, this does not translate into the adoption of a mutual-reward pattern either. Their reaction to being rewarded is thus non-responsive: they do not appear to infer or honor intentions behind others’ rewards. Since rewarders are identifiable to the rewarded, the player could, in principle, single out specific supporters and build dyadic or coalition-based reciprocity. There is no evidence they attempt this. They treat all incoming rewards as purely instrumental gains and maintain the same non-reciprocal, free-riding strategy regardless of which co-players are cooperating or rewarding. This suggests a personality style that is self-focused and strategically myopic with respect to social relationships: they do not exploit the reward structure for alliance-building, reputation management, or norm enforcement, and they do not adjust their strategy as a function of being treated generously. In terms of beliefs, the player likely regards rewards as weak signals that do not require a response, or they may not consider the social implications of ignoring them. Their consistent behavior implies that any norm of “reward those who helped you” or “cooperate more when others support you” is either absent, weak, or overridden by a simple rule of conserving endowment and letting others carry the cooperative burden.

The focal player does not reciprocate with direct rewards to this benefactor, nor do they systematically align their contribution behavior in a way that unmistakably privileges that partner over others. The player does not appear to treat being rewarded as an obligation to “pay back” the specific individual, either with rewards or with preferential cooperative behavior. Their one instance of rewarding is not clearly directed toward the consistent rewarder in a way that establishes a stable alliance pattern; given the data, whether it is intended as gratitude, experimentation, or signaling is unknown. text: Despite being rewarded multiple times by at least one identifiable peer, they never reciprocate with reward. There is no observable tit-for-tat or alliance formation via the reward channel. When another player spends to reward them, they do not adjust their own later behavior in a clearly favoring way toward that specific individual, either via reward or through noticeably higher contributions uniquely aligned with that partner. Their response is thus behaviorally “muted”: they accept rewards passively but do not pay back. This does not mean they ignore social information entirely; they remain generally cooperative in contribution decisions. However, the link between being rewarded and any special treatment of rewarders is absent. The most parsimonious interpretation is that they compartmentalize the reward stage away from their cooperative intentions: they are willing to cooperate in the public good itself, but they do not translate gratitude into costly, directed reciprocation when the mechanism is explicitly optional and individually payoff-neutral. This suggests a personality style that is relatively resistant to social pressure via explicit incentives. They appear not to feel obliged, in behavioral terms, to repay kind acts when repayment is individually costly and not structurally required. Their cooperative stance is therefore driven more by internal norms or general expectations about the group than by dyadic obligations to particular rewarders.

They never reciprocate reward in kind to any partner, regardless of who rewarded them, the size of the reward, or the timing. They also do not appear to reciprocate rewards via the reward mechanism, indicating that they do not use positive reciprocity in the form of costly transfers. Any internal feelings of gratitude are unobservable; behaviorally, they remain fixed on their prior strategy and do not attempt to build bilateral alliances or mutual-reward relationships. Their type is thus characterized by non-reciprocity in the explicit reward channel, while still maintaining unconditional cooperation in contributions.

text: When others reward this player, they do not visibly reciprocate with targeted rewards in return. They continue their own high contributions, but they do not shift from punishment toward reward or engage in partner-specific positive reciprocity. There is no clear sign that being rewarded changes whom they punish or how much they contribute. This suggests they appreciate rewards only implicitly—if at all—and they do not use them as a basis for alliance building or special treatment. Their stance toward rewarders can be summarized as: maintain general cooperation, but do not engage in explicit reciprocal rewarding.

text: When others reward this player, they do not change their long-run behavior in any clear cooperative direction. After being rewarded following cooperative or mixed behavior, they do not shift into a sustained pattern of contributing or into rewarding others in return. Any temporary cooperation appears short-lived and does not develop into a stable, reward-responsive strategy. They also never reciprocate rewards directly: there is no case where someone gives them reward and they, in turn, incur any cost to reward that specific individual or any other cooperator. This implies that gratitude, reciprocity, and alliance building via reward are either low priorities or not integrated into their decision-making in this game. They treat received rewards as unrelated to their own future obligations, either moral or strategic. In social-personality terms (within the game), they present as non-reciprocal and individually focused: they accept benefits and positive signals from others without answering them in kind, and they are comfortable remaining a net beneficiary of others’ cooperation and generosity. They do not appear to infer, from being rewarded, that others desire more contributions from them, or if they do infer it, they do not act on it. Overall, their response to being rewarded is limited to passively collecting the extra payoff, with no detectable attempt to maintain or deepen cooperative relationships.

text: When others reward this player, they continue to free-ride and do not reciprocate through either contributions or rewards. Being rewarded does not trigger any detectable change in behavior, either immediately or over subsequent periods. They neither increase contribution levels nor begin to use the reward mechanism in apparent response to having been identified and singled out as a reward recipient. This suggests that they does not engage in direct reciprocity toward rewarders, nor in generalized reciprocity toward the group. Gratitude, if felt, does not translate into observable cooperative action. They does not treat reward receipt as a signal to join a cooperative “club” or alliance, nor as a cue to conform to a pro-social norm being enforced through rewards. Their response pattern indicates that they interprets rewards as a pure windfall rather than as part of a social exchange. They accepts the benefits but does not adjust strategy, reinforcing the view that their underlying type is payoff-focused and largely unresponsive to social incentives that require sacrificing own coins, even when those incentives are clearly targeted and identifiable.

text: The player repeatedly receives rewards from others yet shows no behavioral change in response; they continue their existing cooperation level and continue to refrain from rewarding anyone. They do not reciprocate rewards to those who reward them, nor do they escalate cooperation (already maximal) or adopt additional pro-social actions such as “thanking” via reciprocal reward. This suggests that, in their behavior, receiving reward is either treated as exogenous noise, as a one-sided benefit, or as something that does not alter their strategy. There is no observable attempt to build bilateral alliances through mutual rewarding, and no sign of conditional reciprocity with respect to the reward channel. If they feel gratitude, it does not manifest in the observable decisions. Their cooperative strategy appears pre-committed and insensitive to whether others recognize or reinforce it. In new PGG environments with identifiable rewarders, this type is likely to keep contributing at a high level regardless of who rewards them, and is unlikely to invest in reciprocity or coalition formation through the reward mechanism.

text: When rewarded, this player accepts the benefit without visible behavioral reciprocation. They do not increase their use of reward, nor do they adopt a more consistently cooperative contribution pattern following receipt of rewards. There is no detectable shift toward favoring high contributors who may have rewarded them, nor any sign of targeted reciprocity. Their response suggests that they treat incoming rewards as windfall gains rather than as part of a social exchange that carries obligations. They do not appear to adopt a “tit-for-tat” or alliance-building approach; they neither reciprocate with rewards nor with systematically higher contributions after having been positively recognized. Because they never reward anyone, their reaction to identifiable rewarders is behaviorally neutral: they neither repay nor punish, and they do not adjust strategic posture in a way that is observable from contributions or reward behavior. Any internal gratitude, if it exists, does not translate into costly action. Overall, they remain individually payoff-focused and largely unresponsive, in strategic terms, to others’ prosocial signals.

text: This player continues to fully cooperate regardless of whether they receive little, moderate, or relatively higher amounts of reward, indicating that their underlying cooperative rule does not depend on being rewarded. They do not escalate their own reward spending in lockstep with changes in reward they receive, so direct reciprocal “you rewarded me, I’ll reward you back” behavior is not clearly present. Because the record does not reveal who rewarded them, it is unknown whether they attempt to reciprocate to specific individuals. Their contribution behavior is stable even when they receive no net advantage from others’ rewards, which suggests that they do not condition their cooperation on personal recognition or alliance benefits. They seem relatively low-reactive: being rewarded does not visibly change their main decision (full contribution), and any shifts in reward-giving are limited and not obviously synchronized with reward receipts. This fits a personality style that values cooperation for its own sake and treats rewards as secondary or experimental rather than as the core basis for alliances or tit-for-tat reciprocity. Whether they feel gratitude internally or attempt subtle alliance building through targeted reward is unknown, but their observable behavior suggests they remain principled and steady rather than strategically reciprocal.

text: This player reliably receives rewards across many rounds, which indicates that others in the group recognize them as a cooperator and/or as someone worth supporting. In response, they do not shift into self-protection or exploitation; instead, they mostly maintain their cooperative and rewarding stance. This suggests that being rewarded affirms their existing approach rather than fundamentally changing it. Their single clear free-riding experiment happens despite prior and ongoing rewards, implying that rewards do not completely immunize them against payoff curiosity or strategic testing. However, once that test is completed, and even though they see that free-riding with no reward cost is lucrative, they return to contributing and resume spending on rewards. That pattern is consistent with a player who experiences rewards as validation of a cooperative identity and who is willing to keep “earning” that identity by continuing to contribute and to reward others. Because individual rewarders and specific reciprocity patterns are not visible in the transcript, their exact response to particular rewarders is unknown. There is no clear evidence that they specifically target those who rewarded them more than others, so strong directed reciprocity toward identifiable rewarders is low-confidence or unknown. The observable pattern is more consistent with generalized reciprocity and group-focused alliance building: being rewarded supports their commitment to the cooperative group as a whole, and they continue to invest in that group through both contributions and rewards.

text: The player consistently continues maximal cooperation after receiving rewards, showing no sign of withdrawal, resentment, or opportunistic adjustment. They neither escalate their own spending on rewards dramatically when receiving more rewards, nor do they switch to exploiting others’ generosity. This indicates that their cooperative behavior is not contingent on being personally rewarded; they cooperate regardless of the presence or absence of reward. Whether they specifically reciprocate toward particular rewarders, or attempt to build alliances with them, is unknown because the identity of their reward targets is not observable. Their limited but nonzero reward spending in the presence of incoming rewards is consistent with mild positive reciprocity or gratitude, but the evidence is ambiguous: the amounts they give and receive are roughly comparable over the whole interaction, which could reflect either reciprocal sentiment or independent use of reward for norm-signaling. They do not use the reward mechanism to retaliate, since only positive transfers are available; within that constraint, they present as steady, reliable, and not particularly strategic in interpersonal maneuvering—more focused on maintaining their own cooperative standard than on managing bilateral relationships.

text: This player is consistently responsive to being rewarded. Even though the transcript does not list exact senders, their behavior changes in ways that suggest they notice and care about received rewards. When they receive substantial positive reinforcement, they are willing to contribute and to continue or even enlarge their own rewarding of others. They do not withdraw or retaliate when rewards to them are low; instead, they still sometimes spend on rewarding others despite relatively modest incoming benefits. Because rewarders are identifiable, it is plausible—but not confirmed—that they selectively reciprocate towards specific high-contributing or high-rewarding partners. The data does show that they keep supporting cooperative profiles over time, which is consistent with alliance-building and emergent coalition thinking, though the exact mapping between who rewards them and whom they reward remains unknown. They appear to interpret rewards as meaningful social signals rather than as mere transfers of coins. Being rewarded does not make them purely altruistic or purely cooperative, but it does seem to stabilize their willingness to invest in the reward system and to keep some level of contribution going. They do not exhibit spiteful reactions to being under-rewarded; there is no sharp drop in cooperative or rewarding behavior after low or zero incoming rewards, which indicates relatively low conditional revenge motive and a moderate, forgiving disposition. Overall, they behave as a socially sensitive but still self-interested player who uses reward and response to reward to manage relationships and group norms, rather than as a rigid reciprocator or a purely payoff-maximizing free-rider.

text: The player receives rewards in multiple rounds but never responds using the available reward channel. They do not reciprocate by rewarding those who reward them, nor do they show any evident behavioral shift in their own contributions that is tightly linked to the receipt of reward. Their contributions remain generally high, but similar levels of cooperation appear both with and without incoming rewards, so their cooperative stance seems driven more by internal norms or beliefs about group efficiency than by gratitude toward identifiable benefactors. Given that rewarders are identifiable to the rewarded, their lack of reciprocal rewarding indicates that they do not treat reward as part of a social exchange or alliance-building process. They neither escalate into mutual-reward relationships nor punish non-rewarders by withholding cooperation. Instead, they appear to keep their contribution strategy largely independent from the specific personal rewards they receive, treating rewards as a bonus rather than as a cue for dyadic reciprocity. Their overall response pattern suggests a personality style that is prosocial in direct contribution but relatively indifferent to interpersonal signaling via rewards. They accept being rewarded without feeling compelled to return the favor, and they do not convert reward into a special tie or in-group relation with any particular co-player. The underlying type is thus “cooperative but non-reciprocal in second-order interactions”: they cooperate for the group, not to maintain or reward specific partners.

text: The player **regularly accepts rewards without any observable attempt to reciprocate or acknowledge them** through their own choices. Because rewarders are identifiable, they could in principle respond by rewarding back or aligning more strongly with those who support them, yet there is no behavioral evidence of such directed reciprocity or alliance building. Their contribution and reward decisions do not systematically shift in response to being rewarded. They neither become more cooperative when others reward them nor begin to reward those who reward them. Instead, rewards appear to be treated as **windfall income**: beneficial but not obligation-creating. This suggests that they do not adopt a “you scratched my back, I’ll scratch yours” norm, even when it would be straightforward to implement. There is thus no sign of **dyadic loyalty** toward rewarders or of any emerging “team” psychology in response to pro-social gestures. Their stance toward rewarders is best characterized as **instrumentally detached**: they are willing to benefit from others’ generosity but do not change their own costly behaviors—either contributions to the public good or use of the reward mechanism—in a way that privileges those rewarders. In summary, when rewarded, this player is **non-retaliatory and non-grateful in behavioral terms**: they do not punish, ignore, or oppose rewarders, but they also do not reciprocate. Their response is essentially neutral: accept benefits, maintain own strategy.

text: When they receive reward, they continue to act cooperatively and maintain or increase contributions rather than shifting toward exploitation, which suggests that being rewarded reinforces their inclination to support the group. There is no evidence that they punish or retaliate against others, and their cooperative behavior persists even when reward inflows are modest or variable, indicating they are not purely quid-pro-quo transactional. Their pattern of rewarding suggests some degree of reciprocity toward those who support them, but the transcript does not reveal exact pairwise flows, so the strength of direct, targeted reciprocity is uncertain (low confidence). It is plausible, but not confirmable, that they preferentially reward those who reward them, forming mutual-support clusters. At minimum, they do not appear to react negatively to being rewarded; instead, they remain engaged, cooperative, and willing to keep investing in others. Socially, they behave like a coalition-oriented cooperator: they respond to reward by continuing to uphold cooperative norms, and they use reward to acknowledge and reinforce allies. They seem comfortable with mutually beneficial exchanges of help and recognition and do not exhibit signs of withdrawing or turning exploitative when identified as a recipient. They accept reward as part of a cooperative signaling system and respond by sustaining, rather than undermining, the emerging cooperative structure.

text: When they receive reward, their observable response is to continue prioritizing self-payoff and free-riding. Being rewarded does not trigger an increase in their contributions, nor does it induce them to start rewarding others. Thus, their response to being rewarded is effectively non-reciprocal. They do not appear to interpret reward as the start of an alliance or cooperative relationship. Even when rewarders are identifiable, they do not use that information to form a cooperative cluster, to match the kindness, or to adjust behavior toward apparent “friends.” There is no evidence of direct reciprocity (rewarding back) or indirect reciprocity (behaving more cooperatively toward the group after being rewarded). Psychologically, this suggests that gratitude or social obligation, if present, is too weak to override their baseline strategy of personal payoff maximization. Alternatively, they may view received reward as a windfall with no associated expectation of behavioral change. Overall, they are a non-responsive recipient of reward: identification of rewarders does not lead to alliance-building, norm reinforcement, or strategy updates, and their type remains that of a robust free-rider regardless of others’ positive social signals.

text: The player reliably receives rewards from others across multiple situations, including when they are both cooperating and when they temporarily free-ride. This indicates that some group members see them as salient or important enough to reward, and the player is aware of this. Their own reward behavior does not collapse after being rewarded; they continue to contribute highly and at times continue to reward others as well. This suggests they interpret incoming rewards as positive feedback and respond by maintaining or modestly enhancing prosocial behavior rather than exploiting that goodwill. When they defect once and still receive some reward, they do not pivot into a long-term free-riding strategy, which indicates that being rewarded does not license opportunism for them. There is no direct evidence of targeted reciprocal reward toward specific previous rewarders (because identities and flows are not given here), so targeted reciprocity toward individual rewarders is unknown. At the aggregate level, however, they behave in a way consistent with generalized reciprocity: being in a rewarding environment coincides with them maintaining high cooperation and periodic reward-giving, as if they are participating in a loose coalition of cooperators rather than playing a purely individualistic strategy.
